slides.heading: The Science of Sleep Cycles
slides.body: Why 8 hours isn't always enough — and what really happens when you sleep

slides.heading: Sleep Isn't One Long Nap
slides.body: Sleep is divided into repeating 90-minute cycles. Each night you go through 4–6 of these cycles, each containing distinct stages with very different functions for your brain and body.

slides.heading: Stage 1 & 2: Light Sleep
slides.body: You drift off and your heart rate slows. Stage 2 brings sleep spindles — brief bursts of brain activity that help consolidate memories and protect sleep from disturbances.

slides.heading: Stage 3: Deep Sleep (Slow-Wave)
slides.body: This is your body's repair mode. Growth hormone is released, tissues are rebuilt, and your immune system is strengthened. It's hardest to wake someone from deep sleep — and most of it happens in the first half of the night.

slides.heading: REM Sleep: The Dream Stage
slides.body: Rapid Eye Movement sleep is when vivid dreams occur. Your brain is nearly as active as when awake, processing emotions, consolidating long-term memories, and boosting creativity. REM dominates the second half of the night.

slides.heading: Why Timing Matters More Than Duration
slides.body: Waking in the middle of deep sleep causes grogginess called sleep inertia. Waking at the end of a 90-minute cycle — when sleep is lightest — leaves you feeling refreshed. Smart alarms use this principle.

slides.heading: The Role of Adenosine & Circadian Rhythm
slides.body: Two systems govern sleepiness. Adenosine builds up in the brain the longer you're awake (caffeine blocks it). Your circadian clock — reset by morning light — determines when you feel alert or drowsy.

slides.heading: What Sleep Deprivation Really Does
slides.body: Even one night of poor sleep impairs judgment, reaction time, and emotional regulation as severely as alcohol intoxication. Chronic deprivation raises risk of heart disease, diabetes, obesity, and cognitive decline.

slides.heading: Tips to Optimize Your Cycles
slides.body: Keep a consistent sleep/wake schedule, even on weekends. Get bright light in the morning and dim lights at night. Avoid caffeine after 2 PM and alcohol close to bedtime — it suppresses REM sleep.

slides.heading: Sleep Smarter, Not Just Longer
slides.body: Quality sleep architecture — cycling through all stages — matters as much as hours. Prioritize consistent schedules and sleep hygiene to wake up genuinely restored every day.
